examples.title: Check if Function is One-to-One
examples.description: Algorithm to verify if a function represented as dictionary is injective
examples.code:
def is_one_to_one(f, domain, codomain):
    """
    Check if function f: domain → codomain is one-to-one
    f is dict mapping elements from domain to codomain
    """
    images = set()
    for x in domain:
        y = f[x]
        if y in images:
            return False  # Two different inputs map to same output
        images.add(y)
    return True

# Example usage
domain = [1, 2, 3]
codomain = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
f = {1: 'a', 2: 'b', 3: 'c'}
print(is_one_to_one(f, domain, codomain))  # True
examples.explanation: We iterate through domain, collect all images. If any image repeats, function is not one-to-one. Uses set to detect duplicates.

examples.title: Check if Function is Onto
examples.description: Algorithm to verify if a function from domain to codomain is surjective
examples.explanation: We collect all images in a set, then check if every element in codomain appears in this set. If any codomain element is missing, function is not onto.

examples.title: Binary Search as Bijective Function
examples.description: Demonstrates bijective property in a finite sorted array mapping indices to values
examples.code:
// Binary Search in sorted array [0..n-1]
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(arr, target)
BEGIN
  left ← 0
  right ← n - 1
  
  WHILE left ≤ right DO
    mid ← (left + right) DIV 2
    
    IF arr[mid] = target THEN
      RETURN mid  // Found - unique position
    ELSE IF arr[mid] < target THEN
      left ← mid + 1
    ELSE
      right ← mid - 1
    END IF
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN -1  // Not found
END

// Property: For sorted array with distinct elements,
// each value maps to unique index → ONE-TO-ONE
// Not necessarily ONTO (not all indices have targets)
examples.explanation: In a sorted array with distinct elements, each value corresponds to exactly one position (one-to-one). Binary search efficiently finds this unique mapping using divide and conquer.

examples.title: Function Composition Implementation
examples.description: Java program demonstrating composition of functions
examples.explanation: Java's Function interface provides compose() method for function composition. gof.apply(x) = g(f(x)). The isBijective check verifies if all inputs map to unique outputs (injective) by comparing set size to domain size.
